verses.text: In his days Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon came up, and Jehoiakim became his servant three years: then he turned and rebelled against him. And the LORD sent against him bands of the Chaldees, and bands of the Syrians, and bands of the Moabites, and bands of the children of Ammon, and sent them against Judah to destroy it, according to the word of the LORD, which he spake by his servants the prophets. Surely at the commandment of the LORD came this upon Judah, to remove them out of his sight, for the sins of Manasseh, according to all that he did; And also for the innocent blood that he shed: for he filled Jerusalem with innocent blood; which the LORD would not pardon. Guidance: The raiding bands — and the deepest cause named. The great power has shifted: Babylon under Nebuchadnezzar now dominates the region (having crushed Egypt at Carchemish in 605 BC). Jehoiakim submits as a vassal for three years, then rebels — a fatal miscalculation. The LORD responds by sending 'bands of the Chaldees [Babylonians], and bands of the Syrians, and bands of the Moabites, and bands of the children of Ammon... against Judah to destroy it, according to the word of the LORD, which he spake by his servants the prophets.' Raiders from every side harass the rebellious kingdom. But the narrator drives past the political surface to the theological depth: 'Surely at the commandment of the LORD came this upon Judah, to remove them out of his sight, for the sins of Manasseh... And also for the innocent blood that he shed: for he filled Jerusalem with innocent blood; which the LORD would not pardon.' The chapter is showing us that the unfolding catastrophe is not mere geopolitics but the LORD's own judgment, executed exactly as the prophets had foretold. Babylon is the rod, but the LORD wields it. And the deepest cause is named again: the sins of Manasseh, and above all the innocent blood he shed, 'which the LORD would not pardon.' The chapter is teaching us that behind the visible churn of empires and rebellions stands the sovereign hand of God, working His declared purposes. The raiding bands did not come by chance; the LORD 'sent' them. The chapter is teaching us, with sobering clarity, that there is such a thing as a sin so grave, so unrepented at the national level, that the LORD 'would not pardon' its consequences — Manasseh's flood of innocent blood had filled up the measure of Judah's guilt. (This does not contradict Manasseh's personal repentance and forgiveness in 2 Chr 33; the man was pardoned, but the national bloodguilt and the people's persistent idolatry brought the temporal judgment that now arrives.) The chapter is teaching us that God's patience, though vast, reaches a limit, and that the shedding of innocent blood is among the sins He holds in most solemn account. The judgment Manasseh's reign earned now begins, at the LORD's command, to fall.

Now the rest of the acts of Jehoiakim, and all that he did, are they not written in the book of the chronicles of the kings of Judah? So Jehoiakim slept with his fathers: and Jehoiachin his son reigned in his stead. And the king of Egypt came not again any more out of his land: for the king of Babylon had taken from the river of Egypt unto the river Euphrates all that pertained to the king of Egypt. Guidance: Jehoiakim's end and Egypt's eclipse. The standard formula closes Jehoiakim's reign, and his son Jehoiachin succeeds. (The simple 'slept with his fathers' obscures a grim end — Jeremiah had prophesied Jehoiakim would have 'the burial of an ass,' dragged out and cast beyond the gates, Jer 22:18-19; the brevity here may reflect the dishonor of his death amid the Babylonian incursions.) The narrator adds a strategically important note: 'the king of Egypt came not again any more out of his land: for the king of Babylon had taken from the river of Egypt unto the river Euphrates all that pertained to the king of Egypt.' Babylon has stripped Egypt of all its former Levantine holdings; Egypt, on which Judah had repeatedly and foolishly leaned, is now powerless to help. The chapter is showing us the closing of every earthly avenue of rescue. Judah had a long, fatal habit of trusting Egypt against the northern powers (a habit the prophets condemned again and again); now Egypt is finished as a regional player, unable to come to Judah's aid. The chapter is teaching us that the worldly powers in which the faithless trust eventually fail them utterly. Judah had spurned trust in the LORD and leaned on Egypt's 'bruised reed'; now that reed is shattered, and Judah stands alone before Babylon. The chapter is teaching us the bankruptcy of all merely human security: the empire Judah counted on is swept from the board, and there is no longer any earthly power to deliver. The kingdom that would not trust the LORD now has nothing and no one else to trust. The stage is cleared for the final act: Jerusalem alone, under the full weight of Babylon, with the LORD's pronounced judgment about to fall.

Jehoiachin was eighteen years old when he began to reign, and he reigned in Jerusalem three months. And his mother's name was Nehushta, the daughter of Elnathan of Jerusalem. And he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, according to all that his father had done. Guidance: Jehoiachin — three months before the first great captivity. Jehoiakim's son Jehoiachin (also called Jeconiah or Coniah) takes the throne at eighteen and reigns just three months. The verdict is the same: 'he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, according to all that his father had done.' His brief reign is a hinge on which the first major deportation turns. (Jeremiah pronounced a bitter judgment on him — Jer 22:24-30 — that though he were a signet ring on the LORD's hand, he would be plucked off and carried to Babylon, and that none of his offspring would prosper on David's throne; yet, remarkably, Jehoiachin appears in the genealogy of Christ in Matthew 1:11-12, and his release at the book's end, 25:27-30, keeps a thread of Davidic hope alive.) The chapter is showing us the relentless continuation of the pattern even in the kingdom's final days: another king, another 'evil in the sight of the LORD,' the dynasty unbroken in its faithlessness even as the end rushes upon it. The chapter is teaching us that the approach of judgment did not produce repentance in Judah's kings — each one, down to the last, simply continued in his fathers' evil. The chapter is also setting up the first great deportation: it is in Jehoiachin's three-month reign that Nebuchadnezzar comes and carries off the young king, the leading citizens, and the temple treasures (the deportation of 597 BC, which took Ezekiel and others to Babylon). The chapter is teaching us the sobering speed of the unfolding catastrophe — the kings now flash by in months, and with each one another piece of Judah's life and treasure is stripped away and carried east.

At that time the servants of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon came up against Jerusalem, and the city was besieged. And Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon came against the city, and his servants did besiege it. And Jehoiachin the king of Judah went out to the king of Babylon, he, and his mother, and his servants, and his princes, and his officers: and the king of Babylon took him in the eighth year of his reign. Guidance: The first siege and Jehoiachin's surrender. Nebuchadnezzar's forces besiege Jerusalem, and Nebuchadnezzar himself comes against it. Faced with the overwhelming Babylonian army, the young king Jehoiachin chooses surrender over a doomed resistance: 'Jehoiachin the king of Judah went out to the king of Babylon, he, and his mother, and his servants, and his princes, and his officers' — the entire royal establishment gives itself up. 'And the king of Babylon took him in the eighth year of his reign' (Nebuchadnezzar's eighth year, 597 BC). The chapter is showing us the first fall of Jerusalem — not yet the final destruction (the city and temple still stand), but the first great surrender and deportation. The royal family and court go out and submit to the conqueror. The chapter is teaching us about the difference between this surrender and the final catastrophe to come: Jehoiachin's submission spared the city from immediate destruction (and his life, as the book's ending will show), whereas his uncle Zedekiah's later rebellion will bring total ruin (ch 25). There is, even in judgment, a measure of relative wisdom in submission versus suicidal defiance — though the chapter does not commend Jehoiachin (he was evil), his surrender at least preserved the city for another decade and his own life for eventual release. The chapter is teaching us that when the LORD's judgment is decreed and the instrument of it stands at the gate, resistance in pride only multiplies the destruction; the kingdom's last hope was never in defiance of Babylon but in repentance toward God — and that hope, the kings would not take. The first deportation is the LORD's judgment beginning to fall in stages, with mercy still mingled in the measure.

And he carried out thence all the treasures of the house of the LORD, and the treasures of the king's house, and cut in pieces all the vessels of gold which Solomon king of Israel had made in the temple of the LORD, as the LORD had said. And he carried away all Jerusalem, and all the princes, and all the mighty men of valour, even ten thousand captives, and all the craftsmen and smiths: none remained, save the poorest sort of the people of the land. And he carried away Jehoiachin to Babylon, and the king's mother, and the king's wives, and his officers, and the mighty of the land, those carried he into captivity from Jerusalem to Babylon. And all the men of might, even seven thousand, and craftsmen and smiths a thousand, all that were strong and apt for war, even them the king of Babylon brought captive to Babylon. Guidance: The first great deportation (597 BC). Nebuchadnezzar strips Jerusalem: he carries off 'all the treasures of the house of the LORD, and the treasures of the king's house, and cut in pieces all the vessels of gold which Solomon king of Israel had made in the temple' — the golden vessels of Solomon's temple, the glory of the LORD's house for nearly four centuries, are cut up and hauled to Babylon, 'as the LORD had said' (fulfilling Isaiah's word to Hezekiah, 20:17). Then the human deportation: 'all Jerusalem, and all the princes, and all the mighty men of valour, even ten thousand captives, and all the craftsmen and smiths: none remained, save the poorest sort of the people of the land.' The leadership, the military, the skilled artisans — the backbone of the nation — are carried into exile; only the poorest are left to till the soil. Jehoiachin himself, with the queen mother, the royal wives, the officers, and the mighty men (7,000 soldiers and 1,000 craftsmen), is taken to Babylon. The chapter is showing us the first stage of the great exile — the carrying-away that the prophets had long foretold and that the narrator marks with the solemn 'as the LORD had said.' Among these 597 BC exiles was the prophet Ezekiel, who would prophesy from Babylon to the captive community. The chapter is teaching us the precise fulfillment of Isaiah's century-old word to Hezekiah: the very treasures Hezekiah had proudly shown the Babylonian envoys (20:13) are now carried to Babylon exactly as Isaiah warned. God's word, however long deferred, comes to pass. The chapter is teaching us the gravity of the loss: the sacred vessels of Solomon's temple, dedicated to the LORD's worship, are cut to pieces by pagan hands — a visceral sign that the LORD was indeed removing His people 'out of his sight.' The chapter is also quietly noting what remained: 'the poorest sort of the people of the land.' Even in this stripping, the LORD left a remnant in the land — though the kingdom's strength was gone. The judgment has fallen heavily; the final blow (ch 25) is yet to come.

God's word, however long deferred, comes to pass. The chapter is teaching us the gravity of the loss: the sacred vessels of Solomon's temple, dedicated to the LORD's worship, are cut to pieces by pagan hands — a visceral sign that the LORD was indeed removing His people 'out of his sight.' The chapter is also quietly noting what remained: 'the poorest sort of the people of the land.' Even in this stripping, the LORD left a remnant in the land — though the kingdom's strength was gone. The judgment has fallen heavily; the final blow (ch 25) is yet to come.

And he carried out thence all the treasures of the house of the LORD, and the treasures of the king's house, and cut in pieces all the vessels of gold which Solomon king of Israel had made in the temple of the LORD, as the LORD had said. And he carried away all Jerusalem, and all the princes, and all the mighty men of valour, even ten thousand captives, and all the craftsmen and smiths: none remained, save the poorest sort of the people of the land. And he carried away Jehoiachin to Babylon, and the king's mother, and the king's wives, and his officers, and the mighty of the land, those carried he into captivity from Jerusalem to Babylon. And all the men of might, even seven thousand, and craftsmen and smiths a thousand, all that were strong and apt for war, even them the king of Babylon brought captive to Babylon. Guidance: The first great deportation (597 BC). Nebuchadnezzar strips Jerusalem: he carries off 'all the treasures of the house of the LORD, and the treasures of the king's house, and cut in pieces all the vessels of gold which Solomon king of Israel had made in the temple' — the golden vessels of Solomon's temple, the glory of the LORD's house for nearly four centuries, are cut up and hauled to Babylon, 'as the LORD had said' (fulfilling Isaiah's word to Hezekiah, 20:17). Then the human deportation: 'all Jerusalem, and all the princes, and all the mighty men of valour, even ten thousand captives, and all the craftsmen and smiths: none remained, save the poorest sort of the people of the land.' The leadership, the military, the skilled artisans — the backbone of the nation — are carried into exile; only the poorest are left to till the soil. Jehoiachin himself, with the queen mother, the royal wives, the officers, and the mighty men (7,000 soldiers and 1,000 craftsmen), is taken to Babylon. The chapter is showing us the first stage of the great exile — the carrying-away that the prophets had long foretold and that the narrator marks with the solemn 'as the LORD had said.' Among these 597 BC exiles was the prophet Ezekiel, who would prophesy from Babylon to the captive community. The chapter is teaching us the precise fulfillment of Isaiah's century-old word to Hezekiah: the very treasures Hezekiah had proudly shown the Babylonian envoys (20:13) are now carried to Babylon exactly as Isaiah warned. God's word, however long deferred, comes to pass. The chapter is teaching us the gravity of the loss: the sacred vessels of Solomon's temple, dedicated to the LORD's worship, are cut to pieces by pagan hands — a visceral sign that the LORD was indeed removing His people 'out of his sight.' The chapter is also quietly noting what remained: 'the poorest sort of the people of the land.' Even in this stripping, the LORD left a remnant in the land — though the kingdom's strength was gone. The judgment has fallen heavily; the final blow (ch 25) is yet to come.

And he carried away all Jerusalem, and all the princes, and all the mighty men of valour, even ten thousand captives, and all the craftsmen and smiths: none remained, save the poorest sort of the people of the land. God's word, however long deferred, comes to pass. The chapter is teaching us the gravity of the loss: the sacred vessels of Solomon's temple, dedicated to the LORD's worship, are cut to pieces by pagan hands — a visceral sign that the LORD was indeed removing His people 'out of his sight.' The chapter is also quietly noting what remained: 'the poorest sort of the people of the land.' Even in this stripping, the LORD left a remnant in the land — though the kingdom's strength was gone. The judgment has fallen heavily; the final blow (ch 25) is yet to come.

And the king of Babylon made Mattaniah his father's brother king in his stead, and changed his name to Zedekiah. Guidance: Zedekiah installed as the last king. Nebuchadnezzar installs Mattaniah, Jehoiachin's uncle (Josiah's son), as a puppet king, changing his name to Zedekiah — the renaming, as with Necho and Jehoiakim, asserting the overlord's sovereignty over his vassal. Zedekiah will be the last king of Judah. (His name, ironically, means 'the LORD is my righteousness' — but his reign will be marked by faithlessness and the final catastrophe; the prophet Jeremiah ministered to him throughout, repeatedly urging submission to Babylon as the LORD's will, counsel Zedekiah feared to follow.) The chapter is showing us Judah reduced to a fully subject vassal-state, its king a Babylonian appointee. The Davidic throne still stands, but only at the pleasure of a pagan emperor. The chapter is teaching us how far the kingdom has fallen from the days of David and Solomon — once an empire in its own right, Judah is now a conquered province whose king is named and unnamed by a foreign master. The chapter is teaching us, in the bitter irony of the name 'Zedekiah' ('the LORD is my righteousness'), the gap between profession and reality: the last king bore a name confessing the LORD's righteousness while leading the kingdom in the final rebellion that would destroy it. The chapter is setting the stage for the climax: with Zedekiah on the throne as Babylon's vassal, the final act depends on whether he will submit (as Jeremiah urged, preserving the city) or rebel (bringing total destruction). The next verses, and chapter 25, will reveal his fatal choice. The last king of David's line in Jerusalem before the exile takes his place — and the countdown to the end begins.

Zedekiah was twenty and one years old when he began to reign, and he reigned eleven years in Jerusalem. And his mother's name was Hamutal, the daughter of Jeremiah of Libnah. And he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, according to all that Jehoiakim had done. For through the anger of the LORD it came to pass in Jerusalem and Judah, until he had cast them out from his presence, that Zedekiah rebelled against the king of Babylon. Guidance: Zedekiah's evil reign and the fatal rebellion. The last king reigns eleven years (597-586 BC) and, like all his recent predecessors, 'did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, according to all that Jehoiakim had done.' Then the narrator gives a profound theological statement about his rebellion: 'For through the anger of the LORD it came to pass in Jerusalem and Judah, until he had cast them out from his presence, that Zedekiah rebelled against the king of Babylon.' Zedekiah's rebellion against Babylon — the human act that triggers the final destruction — is set within the frame of the LORD's anger working out the decreed judgment. The chapter is showing us the mysterious interplay of human responsibility and divine sovereignty at the very end. Zedekiah genuinely chose to rebel (against Jeremiah's repeated counsel to submit), and he bears responsibility for it; yet the narrator says this came to pass 'through the anger of the LORD... until he had cast them out from his presence.' The king's faithless, foolish rebellion was the means by which the LORD's pronounced judgment was carried out. The chapter is teaching us that God's sovereign purposes are accomplished even through the free and culpable choices of sinful men — Zedekiah's rebellion was both his own sin and the instrument of the LORD's decreed casting-out. The chapter is teaching us a hard truth about judgment: when the LORD's anger has determined to remove a people, He may give them over to the very choices that bring their ruin. Zedekiah, refusing the prophet's word, rebelled — and that rebellion, springing from his own hardened heart, fulfilled the LORD's purpose to cast Judah from His presence. The chapter is teaching us the seriousness of refusing God's word through His prophets: Jeremiah told Zedekiah plainly what to do, and the king's failure to heed it brought the catastrophe of chapter 25. The phrase 'cast them out from his presence' is the dreadful inverse of the covenant blessing — the people who would not have the LORD as their God are now removed from before His face. The final destruction is about to unfold.
